title: SEGMENT 12 — The vascularization wall: 200 micrometers and the oxygen problem

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — The vascularization wall: 200 micrometers and the oxygen problem
Now we come to the central unsolved problem of tissue engineering.
Not "a" problem.
The problem.
The one that determines whether everything we've been talking about can ever scale beyond thin sheets and small constructs to full, transplantable organs.
It is the problem of vascularization — getting blood vessels into engineered tissues.
Here is the basic physics of the problem.
Cells need oxygen.
Oxygen reaches cells by diffusion from the nearest blood vessel.
The maximum distance over which oxygen can diffuse, in a living tissue, at a rate fast enough to keep cells alive, is about 200 micrometers.
Two-tenths of a millimeter.
That's a hard, fundamental limit set by the diffusion constant of oxygen in tissue and the metabolic rate of cells.
In your body, this limit is met by an extensive network of capillaries that ensures every cell is within 200 micrometers of a blood vessel.
Your tissues are densely vascularized at the microscale.
A cubic millimeter of cardiac muscle contains literally hundreds of capillaries.
In an engineered tissue, by contrast, there are no blood vessels.
The cells are sitting in a hydrogel.
Oxygen reaches them by diffusion from the surrounding medium.
If the construct is thin — less than 200 micrometers thick — every cell is reachable, and the construct can survive.
If the construct is thicker, the cells in the middle don't get enough oxygen.
They go hypoxic.
They die.
This is the 200-micrometer wall, and it is why every clinical tissue engineering success so far has been a thin tissue.
Skin?
Thin.
A few hundred micrometers.
Cartilage?
In small focal defects, also thin enough that diffusion can work, especially since cartilage is naturally low-metabolism.
Bladder?
The wall is only a few millimeters thick, and after implantation, vessels from surrounding tissue grow in.
Cornea?
Thin.
Engineered bone constructs of significant thickness?
These have been the hard ones, because bone is not naturally avascular and requires its own blood supply.
Heart muscle patches?
Limited to a few cell layers if you don't pre-vascularize them.
The dream of engineering a whole liver, or a whole kidney, or a whole heart, runs straight into this wall.
A liver is centimeters thick.
A heart is centimeters thick.
Without a built-in blood supply that gets oxygen to every cell, none of those organs can survive once they exceed a millimeter or two in any dimension.
The analogy to hold in your head is this.
Anything thicker than 200 micrometers can't get oxygen by diffusion alone.
You need a printed or grown vascular network with the right architecture — branching, tapering, with capillary-scale terminals — that can be perfused with blood.
Building that vascular network, with the right geometry, at the right scale, and getting it to actually carry blood after implantation, is THE bottleneck for organ engineering.
Everything else — the cells, the scaffolds, the bioactives, the printing precision — is, in some sense, a sideshow until vascularization is solved.
There are several active research strategies for the vascularization problem.

The first is pre-vascularization — building the vascular network into the construct before implantation, using endothelial cells co-cultured with the parenchymal cells, sometimes in patterned hydrogel networks that guide the endothelial cells to form tubes.

Pre-vascularized constructs can develop perfusable vascular networks in vitro, and after implantation, those networks anastomose — connect — with the host's blood vessels, getting blood flow going within hours.
The second strategy is in-construct printable vasculature, in which the bioprinting itself lays down channels in the construct that can be lined with endothelial cells and perfused with blood.
We'll talk about this in detail in the next segment.
The third strategy is vascularized assembly from organoids.

Take vascularized organoids — small clumps of tissue with their own little vessel networks — and assemble them into larger structures, allowing the small networks to anastomose into a continuous network across the assembly.
This is a young approach and shows real promise.
None of these strategies has fully solved the problem at organ scale.

But the field is making real progress, and the most exciting recent advances — the ones that point toward a future in which we can actually engineer thick tissues — have come from work on printable vasculature.
Which is exactly what we'll talk about next.
